summary: Asparouhova draws parallels between app developers and open source developers, arguing both are 'toolmakers' facing similar challenges: low revenue despite high adoption, entitled users, and dependence on platforms (Apple's App Store and GitHub) that don't serve their interests. She examines how platform policies constrain monetization options, leading to poor incentives where 'scammy' developers thrive while those who 'respect humanity' struggle. The piece questions whether platforms will ever align with developer interests, suggesting regulation or revolution may be necessary. She concludes that toolmakers play a critical role in human progress and would benefit from working together across their different domains.
key_points: App developers and open source developers face remarkably similar challenges: high adoption but low revenue, entitled users demanding free work, and platforms that don't understand or advocate for them
Platforms like Apple's App Store and GitHub may actually be 'aggregators' that capture economic value for themselves rather than enabling sustainable businesses for developers
Poor pricing models and platform constraints lead to user entitlement—99-cent app complaints mirror demanding open source issue reporters
The endgame differs: successful open source developers get acquihired and go quiet, while app developers remain independent small business owners with more stake in advocacy
Both groups are 'toolmakers' whose work shapes how society operates, deserving more recognition for accelerating human progress
money_quotes: It's unclear whether we should expect platforms to change their policies... perhaps it's naive to assume that platforms will ever act in their users' best interests.
Open source projects on GitHub represent billions of dollars in value creation, but none of that is captured on GitHub.
'Scammy game makers' thrive off of it and are able to succeed, while 'developers who actually respect humanity' can't.

Successful open source developers get 'acquihired' by a company who values their reputation and skills, which has the perverse effect of making them less noisy about how to make a living off of open source.
People tend to become the software they use.
A good tool can come from anywhere. In this way, we're still a delightfully primitive society, connected at internet scale: someone turns a rock into a hammer, then hands it to a friend.
Toolmakers play a critical role in accelerating human progress, and their work ought to be recognized and celebrated.
key_insight: App developers and open source developers are both 'toolmakers' facing the same structural problem: platforms capture the economic value their work creates while offering no path to sustainability.
